method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island's French name is Île Tromelin, and it was formerly known as the Isle of Sand.[1] Its present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine.[1] Tromelin reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of about 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, approximately 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[4] The island is uninhabited apart from a meteorological station, operating since 1949, which monitors cyclones and supports regional weather forecasting.[4] Its terrain has been described as almost desert-like, with sparse low bushes and no trees.[5] Aeolian sedimentation contributes to the island’s topography, and strong winds on Tromelin are concentrated mainly from directions between 100° and 140°.[3]  

The island supports seabird nesting habitat, including masked boobies and red-footed boobies.[1][2] Before rodent eradication, invasive rats had severely damaged the ecosystem, reducing the island’s bird community to only two remaining species and consuming native vegetation.[1][2] In 2005, French scientists and authorities eradicated the rodents using poisoned bait, allowing vegetation recovery and the return of three bird species.[1][2] Tromelin is also described as a sea turtle sanctuary, with conservation efforts focused on protecting nesting turtles from invasive species and habitat threats.[4][5] Despite ecological recovery after rat eradication, its seabirds remain vulnerable to wider threats such as accidental capture in fisheries, overfishing, and changing ocean conditions.[2]

==Fauna and flora==
The island’s native terrestrial fauna is limited, with seabirds forming the dominant animal community and nesting green sea turtles representing the main non-avian species; no native land mammals, reptiles or amphibians are known.[1] After the eradication of Norway rats, mice remained the only introduced mammals recorded on the island.[3] Historical evidence indicates that six to eight seabird species once bred there in large numbers, but by 2005 breeding was largely restricted to two booby species with poor reproductive success.[1] Subsequent monitoring recorded a recovery to seven breeding seabird species by 2022, with breeding pairs increasing from 353 to 4,758, a change attributed mainly to improved nesting success after rat removal.[1] Non-breeding seabirds observed from 2017 to 2021 included terns, skuas, frigatebirds, tropicbirds and the Mascarene petrel, with great and lesser frigatebirds using the island as regular roosting sites.[3] White-tailed and red-tailed tropicbirds have also been observed landing or displaying, suggesting possible future breeding settlement.[3]

The vegetation is sparse and low-growing, adapted to strong winds, salt spray and limited freshwater availability.[1] The native flora was historically simple, consisting chiefly of the littoral shrub Tournefortia argentea, also known as Heliotropium foertherianum or tree heliotrope, together with fewer than ten herbaceous species and no native trees.[1] Rat predation on seeds, seedlings and foliage greatly reduced native plant cover before eradication efforts in 2005–2006.[1] Monitoring after eradication showed a marked recovery, with native flora increasing to seven taxa by 2013 and Tournefortia argentea becoming denser and taller.[1] A few introduced coconut palms persist, but they do not dominate the island’s plant community.[1] Recovery of vegetation and seabird biomass is considered to have wider ecological effects, including increased nutrient transfer to nearby marine ecosystems through seabird guano.[4]

==History==
The island was first recorded by France in the 1720s, when the navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée named it Île de Sable, or “Isle of Sand”.[1]  

In July 1761, the French ship Utile was wrecked near Tromelin while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[2] About 20 sailors and more than 70 enslaved people died in the wreck, with many of the latter trapped below deck after the hatches had been locked or nailed shut.[2] The surviving French crew eventually left the island, but a group of enslaved Malagasy people remained stranded there for 15 years.[2] In 1776, Captain Chevalier de Tromelin, commanding the Dauphine, rescued the remaining survivors: seven women and a child born on the island.[2][3] The survivors were taken to Mauritius and later freed, and the island was subsequently named in honour of Tromelin.[3]  

Archaeological work in the 21st century has documented the castaways’ long occupation of the island.[2] A French archaeological and diving team found the remains of dwellings built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing traces of turtles, birds and shellfish.[2] Earlier British sailors had also reported seeing the remains of “stone” houses and carefully arranged graves on the island.[2] The archaeological investigations were supported by UNESCO as part of a commemoration of the struggle against slavery.[2]  

France’s sovereignty claim is traced to the arrival of the Dauphine on 29 November 1776.[1] Mauritius disputes French sovereignty, arguing that Tromelin should have passed to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and therefore should not have remained administered as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1] France established a meteorological presence and landing strip on the island in the mid-20th century, reinforcing its effective administration.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate (Köppen climate classification Aw), with warm temperatures year-round and limited seasonal variation because of its low latitude in the western Indian Ocean.[2] The mean annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, and monthly temperatures remain relatively uniform throughout the year.[2] Rainfall is seasonal, with a wetter period from November to April associated with the intertropical convergence zone and a drier period from May to October.[2] Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, with the highest monthly totals generally occurring during the austral summer, especially around February.[2] The island is strongly influenced by the southeast trade winds, which are frequent and can exceed 8 m/s, contributing to high evaporation and persistently exposed conditions.[2] Its position makes it useful for monitoring tropical cyclones in the south-west Indian Ocean, particularly systems that may affect Madagascar, and the island’s weather station continues to support regional forecasting.[2][4] As a low-lying coral island, Tromelin is also vulnerable to cyclone impacts and sea-level-related hazards, although detailed local measurements of sea-level rise remain limited because of its remoteness.[2]
